params.text:
What can go wrong when you are alone

When no colleague is beside you, ordinary hazards can become more serious simply because there is no one to help or raise the alarm. Understanding the range of risks is the first step to staying safe.

params.alt: A care worker arriving for a home visit

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Common risks that lone care workers face. Tap each card to reveal an example.

dialogs.answer: Verbal abuse, threats or physical aggression from the person you support, a relative, a visitor or a member of the public, with no colleague nearby to step in.
You could slip, fall or be taken ill with no one there to notice or call for help. A minor injury can become serious if you cannot summon assistance.
Cluttered or poorly lit spaces, faulty wiring, hoarding, smoke, damp, unsafe stairs or trip hazards that you have no control over and may not expect.
Dogs and other pets can be unpredictable, especially with an unfamiliar visitor. An agitated animal can block your exit or cause injury.
Long hours driving, unfamiliar routes, poor weather, parking in isolated areas and walking alone after dark all add risk to community work.
A first visit means you do not yet know the layout, who else is present, or how the person may react. The unknown is itself a risk to manage.
